title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: development HydraR guided need robust, reproducible, scalable framework agentic orchestration R, specifically scientific bioinformatics research. document outlines key design decisions trade-offs made development.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: One primary design choices strict separation Orchestrator (AgentDAG) Executors (AgentDriver). Logic: DAG manages flow state decision-making graph. Execution: Drivers handle actual interaction external LLMs local tools. Trade-: adds complexity R6 class hierarchy ensures package provider-agnostic. can swap Claude-based driver Gemini-based one without changing workflow logic.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: Unlike many frameworks prioritize latest REST API wrappers, HydraR prioritizes CLI interactions. ?: Scientific environments often limited egress require specific local environment discovery (like path resolution) CLIs handle better raw HTTP calls. CLIs like gemini-cli claude-code also manage authentication retry logic robustly. Trade-: requires user specific CLI tools installed, significantly reduces maintenance burden R package .

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: tasks involving file modifications (e.g., automated code fixing), HydraR uses Git Worktrees provide isolated execution environments. ?: Orchestrating multiple agents parallel directory leads race conditions inconsistent states. Worktrees allow agent (node) operate branch/copy repository without interfering others. Design Choice: chose Worktrees Docker containers keep framework “R-native” avoid overhead container management HPC environments.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: HydraR uses AgentState backed high-performance SQL (DuckDB SQLite). Decision: Moving away complex nested R lists tabular/relational state model ensures checkpoints durable can audited run. Merit: enables “Time-Travel Debugging” researcher can inspect exact state workflow given node transition.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: mermaid_parser.R integration designed make complex agentic graphs “human-readable” default. Philosophy: researcher visualize agent’s logic, trust . supporting round-trip Mermaid syntax, allow users design visual editor execute R, vice versa.

title: Design Thinking in HydraR
text: integrated Validation Engine checks undefined nodes circular dependencies execution starts. Impact: prevents expensive mid-run failures long-running agentic pipelines.

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: Every driver must implement call() method, sends prompt LLM returns text response.
code: library(HydraR) library(R6)  CustomDriver <- R6::R6Class("CustomDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     call = function(prompt, model = NULL, cli_opts = list(), ...) {       # Custom logic to call your provider       return("Response from custom provider")     }   ) )

title: Creating Custom Agent Drivers
text: developing workflows, often want avoid cost latency real LLM calls. Mock Driver provides deterministic responses.
code: MockDriver <- R6::R6Class("MockDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     responses = list(),     call = function(prompt, ...) {       if (length(self$responses) == 0) return("Default")       res <- self$responses[[1]]       self$responses <- self$responses[-1]       return(res)     }   ) )

title: HydraR: The Complete Guide
text: HydraR built around three concepts: Nodes — Individual units work. node might call LLM, run R function, route execution based condition. Edges — Connections nodes define order execution. Edges can unconditional (“always go next”) conditional (“go test passed, otherwise loop back”). State — shared, centralized object every node can read write . Think clipboard gets passed around workflow. Together, form AgentDAG (Directed Acyclic Graph—though HydraR also supports cycles looping).

text: single node isn’t exciting. power HydraR comes connecting nodes. Let’s build two-step pipeline: one node fetches data, next summarises . state flows: fetcher returns output, HydraR automatically stores AgentState key "fetcher". next node, summariser, can retrieve state$get("fetcher"). fundamental mechanism—every node’s output stored ID.
code: library(HydraR)  # Step 1: Fetch data fetch_data <- function(state) {   # Simulate pulling data from a source   raw <- data.frame(     gene = c("TP53", "BRCA1", "EGFR"),     expression = c(12.4, 8.7, 15.1)   )   list(status = "success", output = raw) }  # Step 2: Summarise the data summarise_data <- function(state) {   # Read the output from the previous node   data <- state$get("fetcher")   top_gene <- data[which.max(data$expression), "gene"]   summary_text <- sprintf(     "Analysed %d genes. Highest expression: %s (%.1f)",     nrow(data), top_gene, max(data$expression)   )   list(status = "success", output = summary_text) }  # Build the DAG dag <- AgentDAG$new() dag$add_node(AgentLogicNode$new(id = "fetcher", logic_fn = fetch_data)) dag$add_node(AgentLogicNode$new(id = "summariser", logic_fn = summarise_data))  # Connect them: fetcher runs first, then summariser dag$add_edge("fetcher", "summariser")  # Compile and run dag$compile() results <- dag$run(initial_state = list())  print(results$results$summariser$output) # [1] "Analysed 3 genes. Highest expression: EGFR (15.1)"

Explain findings for a general audience.",   driver = driver,   prompt_builder = function(state) {     paste("Please explain this finding:", state$get("data_prep"))   } ))  # Connect them dag$add_edge("data_prep", "interpreter") dag$compile()  results <- dag$run(initial_state = list()) cat(results$results$interpreter$output)

what: The Pattern: Generate → Validate → Loop or Continue
title: HydraR: The Complete Guide
text: Key takeaway: test function inspects output node. returns TRUE, execution follows if_true (can NULL stop workflow). FALSE, follows if_false (loops back case). max_steps parameter acts safety net prevent infinite loops.
code: library(HydraR)  # A mock driver for testing (no API key needed) MockDriver <- R6::R6Class("MockDriver",   inherit = AgentDriver,   public = list(     call_count = 0,     initialize = function(id = "mock") { super$initialize(id) },     call = function(prompt, ...) {       self$call_count <- self$call_count + 1       if (self$call_count >= 2) {         return("The answer is 42.")       }       return("I'm not sure yet.")     }   ) )  driver <- MockDriver$new() dag <- AgentDAG$new()  # Node 1: The LLM generates an answer dag$add_node(AgentLLMNode$new(   id = "Generator",   role = "Answer the user's question concisely.",   driver = driver,   prompt_builder = function(state) state$get("question") ))  # Node 2: A validator checks the answer dag$add_node(AgentLogicNode$new(   id = "Validator",   logic_fn = function(state) {     answer <- state$get("Generator")     is_good <- grepl("42", answer)     list(status = "success", output = list(passed = is_good))   } ))  # Connect: Generator → Validator dag$set_start_node("Generator") dag$add_edge("Generator", "Validator")  # Add the loop: if validation fails, go back to Generator dag$add_conditional_edge(   from = "Validator",   test = function(result) isTRUE(result$passed),   if_true = NULL,       # NULL means "stop — we're done"   if_false = "Generator" # loop back )  dag$compile() results <- dag$run(   initial_state = list(question = "What is the meaning of life?"),   max_steps = 10 )  cat("Final answer:", results$state$get("Generator")) # Final answer: The answer is 42. cat("\nLLM was called", driver$call_count, "times") # LLM was called 2 times

library(HydraR)  # ── 1. Register reusable logic ── register_logic("parse_results", function(state) {   raw <- state$get("LLMAnalyst")   has_stats <- grepl("p-value|confidence", raw, ignore.case = TRUE)   list(status = "success", output = list(quality_ok = has_stats)) })  register_role("gene_analyst",   "You are a bioinformatics researcher. When analysing gene expression data,    always report fold changes, p-values, and confidence intervals." )  # ── 2. Build the DAG ── driver <- GeminiAPIDriver$new(api_key = Sys.getenv("GOOGLE_API_KEY")) dag <- AgentDAG$new()  dag$add_node(AgentLogicNode$new(   id = "DataLoader",   logic_fn = function(state) {     list(status = "success",          output = "Genes: TP53 (FC=2.3, p=0.001), BRCA1 (FC=0.8, p=0.42)")   } ))  dag$add_node(AgentLLMNode$new(   id = "LLMAnalyst",   role = get_role("gene_analyst"),   driver = driver,   prompt_builder = function(state) {     paste("Interpret these gene expression results:
", state$get("DataLoader"))   } ))  dag$add_node(AgentLogicNode$new(   id = "QualityGate",   logic_fn = get_logic("parse_results") ))  # ── 3. Wire the edges ── dag$set_start_node("DataLoader") dag$add_edge("DataLoader", "LLMAnalyst") dag$add_edge("LLMAnalyst", "QualityGate")  dag$add_conditional_edge(   from = "QualityGate",   test = function(out) isTRUE(out$quality_ok),   if_true = NULL,          # Done — quality passed   if_false = "LLMAnalyst"  # Try again )  # ── 4. Run with checkpointing ── saver <- DuckDBSaver$new(db_path = "gene_analysis.duckdb")  results <- dag$run(   initial_state = list(),   checkpointer = saver,   thread_id = "gene-expr-001",   max_steps = 10 )  cat("Status:", results$status, "
") cat("Final analysis:
", results$state$get("LLMAnalyst"))

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: Modern agentic workflows often involve multiple specialized agents working different parts codebase simultaneously. agents modify files, risk stepping ’s toes—causing merge conflicts corrupted git states. HydraR addresses leveraging Git Worktrees isolated execution. guide demonstrates parallel workflow : 1. Three Agents simultaneously write R code different sorting algorithms (Bubble, Quick, Merge sort) separate worktrees. 2. Merge Harmonizer automatically merges independent branches back main repository. 3. Benchmarking Node executes generated code compare performance. 4. Visualization Node plots results.

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: demonstrate power worktrees, first create temporary Git repository.
code: library(HydraR) library(withr) library(ggplot2) library(future) library(furrr)  # Check for Anthropic API Key (Needed for refined agents) # Must be done BEFORE future::plan so workers inherit the environment if (Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY") == "") {   if (file.exists("../.Renviron")) readRenviron("../.Renviron")   if (file.exists(".Renviron")) readRenviron(".Renviron") }  if (Sys.getenv("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY") == "") {   stop("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not found. Please set it in your .Renviron or workspace.") }  # Ensure Gemini CLI path is configured (Environment variables are inherited by workers) Sys.setenv(HYDRAR_GEMINI_PATH = "/opt/homebrew/bin/gemini")  # 0. Setup Parallel Execution (for Worktree Isolation) future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 3) options(future.rng.onMisuse = "ignore")  # 1. Create a temporary folder repo_root <- file.path(tempdir(), "sorting-benchmark-repo") if (dir.exists(repo_root)) unlink(repo_root, recursive = TRUE) dir.create(repo_root)  # 2. Initialize Git and a README withr::with_dir(repo_root, {   system2("git", c("init"))   system2("git", c("config", "user.email", "apaf@example.com"))   system2("git", c("config", "user.name", "APAF Agent"))   system2("git", c("config", "commit.gpgsign", "false"))   writeLines("# Sorting Benchmark", "README.md")   system2("git", c("add", "README.md"))   system2("git", c("commit", "-m", "Initial_commit"))   system2("git", c("branch", "-M", "main")) })

title: Parallel Benchmarking with Git Worktrees
text: Rather registering roles logic manually R, now define entire workflow architecture (graph, prompts, logic) single declarative YAML file. follows APAF-Agentic-Standard Zero-R-Code definitions.
code: # 1. Load the workflow manifest wf <- load_workflow("sorting_benchmark.yml")  # 2. Extract components mermaid_graph <- wf$graph initial_state <- wf$initial_state  # 3. Inject dynamic environment variables into initial state initial_state$repo_root <- repo_root initial_state$output_dir <- file.path(getwd(), "..", "paper", "figures")  # 4. Instantiate and Compile dag <- AgentDAG$from_mermaid(mermaid_graph, node_factory = auto_node_factory()) compiled_dag <- dag$compile()  # 5. Run with Worktree Isolation # Ensure workers inherit our environment (for API keys and PATH) future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 3)  results <- compiled_dag$run(   initial_state = initial_state,   use_worktrees = TRUE,   repo_root = repo_root,   fail_if_dirty = FALSE,   packages = c("withr", "HydraR", "ggplot2") )  # 6. Save Execution Trace compiled_dag$save_trace("sorting_trace.json")

text: Think “Agent” AI-powered assistant can perform specific tasks. Instead just “chatting” AI, HydraR allows link multiple assistants together Workflow. create “blueprint” (visual map) research tasks. title: Stateful Agentic Orchestration for Scientific Reproducibility
text: Standard agentic frameworks often rely heavily brittle API wrappers volatile state. HydraR addresses three fundamental challenges scientific AI orchestration: 🔍 Auditability: maintains central memory system continuously saves workflow checkpoints (using DuckDB database), ensuring complete, verifiable record AI interactions data changes. 🛡️ Filesystem Safety: leveraging Git worktrees, HydraR creates temporary, isolated project branches agent working parallel. prevents file corruption race conditions multiple tasks run simultaneously. 🏗️ Portable Workflows: defining agent logic using standard YAML files Mermaid.js diagrams, HydraR separates workflow’s design code runs . text-based blueprint acts “OpenAPI Agents”—shareable format easy audit version-control.

title: Stateful Agentic Orchestration for Scientific Reproducibility
text: help researchers different backgrounds, key technical terms used HydraR: Agent: autonomous AI entity (powered LLM) can perform specific tasks make decisions. Workflow: collection tasks (nodes) linked together achieve larger goal. DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph): mathematical structure used represent workflows tasks move specific direction without looping back infinitely. State Machine: system keeps track current “status” moves states based inputs task results. Checkpointing: process saving entire state workflow database can resumed later interrupted. Git Worktree: specialized Git feature allows HydraR create separate, temporary version project agent work , preventing overwriting main files accidentally.

title: Stateful Agentic Orchestration for Scientific Reproducibility
text: HydraR stateful orchestrator, low-level API wrapper. many excellent packages focus communication layer, HydraR focuses lifecycle, state, file-system isolation multi-agent workflows. ellmer: Excellent high-level conversational/chat interfaces. HydraR can use ellmer backend driver individual agents within larger managed graph. mall: Provides concise syntax “mapping” LLM calls data. HydraR designed complex research pipelines require memory, branching logic, error recovery. gptstudio: Tools IDE-centric coding assistance. HydraR built reproducible, non-interactive pipelines automation can run independently RStudio IDE. LangGraph / CrewAI (Python): Python equivalents. HydraR provides native R alternative integrates directly R’s statistical visualization ecosystems without “translation cost” using reticulate core logic.

title: Stateful Agentic Orchestration for Scientific Reproducibility
text: 📍 Graph Orchestration: Define complex agentic workflows using AgentDAG support parallel execution (furrr) conditional loops. 💾 Centralized State: AgentState provides single source truth nodes, support complex reducers history management. 🕒 Persistent Checkpointing: Resumable execution threads via Checkpointer (supporting SQLite/DuckDB). 🖥️ CLI-First Drivers: High-fidelity drivers local provider-based CLIs (Gemini, Claude, OpenAI), ensuring tool calls robust. 📊 Mermaid Visualization: Export agent’s logic directly Mermaid.js syntax interactive documentation. 🛡️ Validation Engine: Integrated compile-time checks undefined nodes, circular dependencies, unreachable states.

Workflow Lifecycle: Documented full “Low Code” lifecycle declarative Mermaid/YAML definitions compiled executed DAGs. Resolved syntax error GeminiImageDriver. Fixed Roxygen parsing errors method titles merged descriptions man/AgentDAG.Rd. Restored flexible function lookup auto_node_factory maintain backward compatibility global functions tests. Clean Registry: Package now achieves perfectly clean R CMD check (0 Errors, 0 Warnings, 0 Notes) core builds. Complete Instruction Manual: Rewrote vignettes/manual.md 16-part narrative-style beginner guide, progressing single “hello world” node loops, YAML workflows, checkpointing, git worktree isolation. Includes direct side--side comparison showing YAML-first approach reduces 40-line R script 4 lines.
